ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for isotonic sports drink as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one 500 ml bottle, equivalent to approximately 500 g, commonly consumed during or after moderate-to-vigorous physical activity.

nutrient_source.title: Isotonic Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for isotonic drink (CR ID D05020006). All core macros and secondary nutrients are as reported.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Glucose polymer sports drink / isotonic carbohydrate-electrolyte beverage
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 78 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for isotonic carbohydrate-electrolyte sports drinks (glucose-based, ~6% carbohydrate solution). The HPB-reported formulation (5.7 g carbohydrate/100 ml, predominantly glucose/sucrose) is consistent with this reference class. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (78 × 28.5) / 100 = 22.2, rounded to 22.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.7 g sugar/100 ml; sodium is low at 41 mg/100 ml but AHA 2018 guidelines flag SSBs as independently raising cardiovascular and blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid isotonic drinks during sedentary periods; if used for post-exercise rehydration, limit volume to the minimum required and prefer zero-sugar electrolyte alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage delivering 5.7 g rapidly absorbed sugar per 100 ml with zero dietary fibre, producing a high glycemic index (~78) and rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid isotonic drinks as a routine beverage; plain water or zero-sugar electrolyte tablets dissolved in water are the preferred alternatives for hydration and electrolyte replacement.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and cardiovascular event risk; sodium content is low but the sugar load is the primary concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit isotonic drink consumption to brief post-exercise recovery windows only and should not use them as a daily hydration source.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar with zero fibre and no anti-inflammatory components; regular SSB intake promotes systemic inflammatory mediators relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should replace isotonic drinks with plain water or unsweetened green tea, as the added sugar content promotes pro-inflammatory pathways without any offsetting anti-inflammatory nutrients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: An isotonic drink is a commercially formulated, ready-to-drink sports beverage designed to match the osmolality of human blood plasma (approximately 280–320 mOsm/kg), enabling rapid fluid and electrolyte absorption during and after physical activity. It typically contains water, sugar (glucose, sucrose, or fructose), sodium chloride, potassium chloride, and citric acid, with natural or artificial flavouring. The Singapore HPB Foodo database records this product at 24 kcal per 100 ml, with 5.7 g carbohydrate (all as sugar) and 41 mg sodium per 100 ml, and zero protein, fat, and dietary fibre. The taste is lightly sweet with a mild salty-citrus note; the texture is thin and watery, similar to diluted fruit juice. Clinically, isotonic drinks are classified as sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs) and are not recommended for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease outside of supervised exercise recovery contexts. The high glycemic index (~78) means blood glucose rises rapidly after consumption. For routine hydration, plain water is preferred. Isotonic drinks are widely consumed in Singapore and globally as sports recovery beverages, sold under brands such as Pocari Sweat, 100Plus, and Gatorade.
common_user_queries: What is the sugar content of isotonic drinks?
Is isotonic drink good for hypertension?
Is Pocari Sweat an isotonic drink?
Can I drink isotonic drinks every day?
Is isotonic drink suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D05020006), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are as reported with confidence 0.95. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using a directly analogous isotonic carbohydrate-electrolyte beverage reference.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero as this is a carbohydrate-electrolyte beverage containing no protein by formulation. fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (D05020006). saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; isotonic drinks contain no fibre by formulation. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this generic isotonic drink are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry D05020006 as of 2026. Potassium is a known ingredient in isotonic formulations but the specific mg value is not reported in the HPB source; a -1 sentinel is used rather than an estimate. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for isotonic carbohydrate-electrolyte beverages (~6% carbohydrate, glucose/sucrose base); the HPB formulation at 5.7 g/100 ml is consistent with this reference class. Glycemic load per 500 ml serving = (78 × 28.5) / 100 = 22.2, reported as 22.3. This food is classified as an SSB (sugar-sweetened beverage) under Singapore HPB and AHA 2018 definitions. Dietary tags low_sodium and low_calorie from the input record are retained as contextually accurate for the per-100 ml values but do not override the SSB classification for health suitability purposes.
